items.q: Why does 'qué' have an accent in '¿Qué quieres?' but not always elsewhere?
items.correct: The accent distinguishes interrogative/exclamative 'qué' from relative 'que'.
items.why: Students see two spellings of the 'same word'. Spanish uses written accents to distinguish homophones with different functions: qué (question/exclamation) vs que (that/which - relative).

items.q: How do you say 'I don't see anything' in Spanish?
items.correct: No veo nada (double negative is correct).
items.why: Students avoid 'double negatives' as in English. Spanish REQUIRES them when nada/nadie/nunca follows the verb: 'no...nada', 'no...nadie', 'no...nunca'. Only one negative if it precedes ('Nada veo').

items.q: How do you say 'I am watching TV right now' emphasising the action is ongoing?
items.correct: Estoy viendo la televisión ahora mismo.
items.why: Students use simple present. For ONGOING actions in progress, use ESTAR + GERUNDIO (-ando/-iendo): estoy comiendo, estoy estudiando, estoy hablando. Note: estar, not ser. Useful for describing current activities in tech/free time topics.
Correct answer: Digital divide. “Broken phone” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: IA. “AI” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximate percentage of Spanish households with internet?
items.why: The real figure is 92. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
